channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Trump Is Taking a Page Out of Iran’s Playbook
item.description: The White House vows to charge all commercial ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Russian Energy Is Now at Ukraine’s Mercy
item.description: Kyiv’s drone offensive against Russian oil facilities has found a sixth gear.

item.title: The stubborn scarcity of female breadwinners
item.description: Wives who outearn their husbands remain rare in America

item.title: How high can Red Bull fly?
item.description: The world’s most formidable marketing machine faces growing threats

item.title: Ukraine’s army gets a performance review
item.description: New defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov is trying to reform the army. The generals are unimpressed

item.title: A squeezed China is trying to wring more from its state assets
item.description: Its latest campaign won’t solve its debt woes, but inches in the right direction

item.title: Stop Overreacting to China’s Missile Test
item.description: It was a milestone for Beijing’s nuclear forces—but not necessarily the provocation many analysts have made it out to be.

item.title: Washington Shouldn’t Fear NATO Expansion
item.description: New allies bring powerful assets—whatever Donald Trump thinks.

item.title: Xi Jinping’s ‘Forging’ of ‘Ethnic Unity and Progress’
item.description: The new law consolidates a decades-long endeavor by the party-state to fundamentally remake ethnic minority policy.

item.title: Afghanistan’s Former Spy Chief on Russia, China, and the Taliban’s Legitimization Strategy
item.description: Masoud Andarabi, previously Afghanistan’s interior minister, says that “Afghans will see the world through Chinese algorithms.”

item.title: The Mother of All Economic Shocks Is Chinese Mercantilism
item.description: Because analysts tend to put the United States at the center of the global economic story, they have overlooked the single most important force of the past 50 years. But it will be obvious to later generations that Chinese mercantilism has been more globally consequential than any other economic shock or policy choice.

item.title: Letting Russia Burn
item.description: From relentless Ukrainian drone strikes to soaring inflation and fuel shortages, Russia is paying an ever-higher price for Vladimir Putin's war of choice. But the war has become the organizing principle of Putin’s regime, leaving no path to lasting peace without a fundamental political transformation.

item.title: E20 Debate Reveals India’s Uneven Energy Transition Trade-offs
item.description: India’s ethanol push boosts energy security and farm incomes, but consumers remain unconvinced amid mileage and price concerns.

item.title: Expanding Export Control to ‘Remote Access’ May Backfire on US AI Ambitions
item.description: The proposed Remote Access Security Act (RASA) could end up delivering Chinese cloud operators the opportunity to displace U.S. tech around the world.

item.title: Is Aung San Suu Kyi dead?
item.description: Myanmar’s jailed leader has not been seen since 2022

item.title: America’s other elections problem
item.description: No other rich democracy hands as many decisions directly to its voters

item.title: South Korea and Mongolia Enter a ‘Golden Era’ of Partnership
item.description: Lee’s state visit emphasized a new level of Mongolia-South Korea ties in a changing regional geopolitical environment.

item.title: 10 Years Ago, the Philippines Won a Major Victory in the South China Sea. Did It Matter?
item.description: What did the arbitral tribunal's award actually achieve?

item.title: Will Lula Win the Century?
item.description: Brazil’s president is democratic, successful, and globally respected.

item.title: What AI Governance Demands
item.description: AI governance must account for everything from the technology’s material requirements to its effects on communities, businesses, labor markets, the environment, and strategic competition. To this end, a framework of verification-based trust—broader than national regulation and more flexible than a classic treaty—is essential.

item.title: NATO Is Still a Sick Puppy
item.description: The Ankara summit only kicked the can down the road.

item.title: A Japanese Wake-Up Call for America
item.description: Japan’s deepening currency and bond-market woes should be a wake-up call for other countries that appear to be on unsustainable fiscal paths, not least the United States, as well as France, Italy, and the United Kingdom. After all, a crisis in one country often draws investors' attention to others facing similar problems.

item.title: ASEAN Foreign Ministers Hold ‘Icebreaker’ Meeting With Myanmar Counterpart
item.description: The Southeast Asian bloc reaffirmed its adherence to its Five-Point Consensus initiative, which was last week rejected by Myanmar’s military-dominated parliament.

item.title: The Politicization of the U.S. Military Did Not Start With Trump
item.description: Nostalgia about the U.S. armed forces being above the partisan fray is not borne out by history.

item.title: America and Iran’s Strange Moment of Opportunity
item.description: How a deadlocked conflict can pave the way for peace.

item.title: Senegal Is on the Brink
item.description: The IMF, the World Bank, and the debt crisis that imperils West Africa.

item.title: Malaysian PM’s Coalition Suffers Significant Defeat in Johor State Election
item.description: Barisan Nasional, the Grand Old Party of Malaysian politics, has been aided by a growing disillusionment with Anwar's leadership and discord among its Malay-Muslim rivals.
